source: Gemini Language Data PDF - The Socio-Semantic Landscape of Non-Standard English
documentTitle: An Exhaustive Analysis of Dialectal Variation, Subcultural Lexicons, and Digital Evolution

aave_aal.categoryName: African American Vernacular English / African American Language

terms.definition: Dry skin (visible grayish tone on darker skin)
terms.etymology: Literal description of dry skin appearance

terms.definition: Term of endearment ("before anyone else" backronym)
terms.etymology: Shortened form of 'babe' or 'baby', later given backronym 'before anyone else'
From gambling terminology indicating confidence in outcome

terms.definition: Extra emotional labor/representation burden on Black individuals in predominantly white spaces
terms.context: Workplace discussions, academic settings, social commentary
terms.etymology: Metaphorical extension of economic taxation to cultural burden
Phonological reduction: fixing to → fixin' to → finna

terms.definition: Nape of neck where hair is tightest/curliest
terms.context: Hair care discussions, Black hair culture
terms.etymology: Metaphorical reference to hottest part of house

terms.definition: Witty insult that exposes truth about someone
terms.etymology: From 'reading someone for filth' - analyzing and exposing flaws

terms.definition: Act of delivering witty, truth-based insults
terms.context: Ballroom culture, drag culture, Black queer spaces
terms.etymology: Gerund form of 'read' - analyzing someone critically

terms.definition: Describing someone as pathetic or pitiful (not emotionally sad)
terms.etymology: Semantic shift from emotional state to value judgment

terms.context: Reported speech, emphasizing what was stated

terms.etymology: Grammaticalization from locative verb to aspect marker
Semantic shift from density to body shape appreciation
Orthographic variation for emphasis and humor
Archaic English form preserved in Appalachia

terms.context: "There's nary a thing wrong with it"
Referring to children or small creatures
terms.etymology: Semantic extension from nasal mucus to small entities

terms.context: "What kind of coke you want?" (meaning any soda)
terms.etymology: Coca-Cola brand name generalized to entire category

terms.context: Describing simplicity or lack of refinement
terms.etymology: From cornpone (simple corn bread) used metaphorically
Semantic shift from spatial to temporal meaning
From 'fixing to' - preparing to do something

terms.definition: Mess, sticky substance; to handle clumsily

terms.etymology: Semantic shift from physical sickness to bad disposition
Possibly from common name used generically
Semantic shift from sled to winter headwear

terms.definition: Command to relax, calm down (from Spanish 'agüitar' - to worry)
terms.context: Telling someone to take it easy

terms.definition: Later, in a bit (Spanish 'in a while')
terms.etymology: Direct borrowing from Spanish temporal phrase

terms.context: Informal address or reference to person

terms.etymology: Possibly from Spanish 'pato' (duck) or 'vato' dialectal form
From Taíno indigenous name for Puerto Rico

terms.context: Term of endearment for close associate
terms.etymology: Spanish 'carnal' (related by blood) extended to chosen family
Spanish term for foreigner, especially French/American

terms.definition: To goof around, waste time (from English 'goof')

terms.etymology: From mamey fruit (soft, easy to eat)
Shortened from 'qué es lo que hay' (what is there)

terms.definition: Suit, formal clothing (zoot suit era term)

terms.etymology: From Spanish 'tigre' (tiger) - metaphor for sharp person

terms.context: Referring to unspecified object or situation
Identity discussions in UK Asian communities

terms.definition: Asian Baby Girl/Gangster (stereotype of party-oriented Asian woman)
Chinese term for 'white left' (naive Western liberals)
terms.etymology: Mandarin 白左 (bái zuǒ) - white + left

terms.definition: Asian American perceived as performatively progressive
terms.context: Internal community critique of political posturing

terms.etymology: Acronym from historical immigration arrival method

terms.definition: Good game (gaming); also grandfather/grandmother in Chinese contexts
terms.etymology: Gaming acronym; Mandarin 哥哥/姑姑 (gēge/gūgu) phonetic match
Musical key metaphor for subdued expression

terms.definition: Stereotype of Asian American hypebeast male
terms.context: Social commentary on Asian American fashion/culture
terms.etymology: Common Vietnamese American name used archetypally

terms.definition: Person known primarily from rave/EDM scene
永远的神 (eternal god) - ultimate praise
Leave it alone, forget about it

terms.etymology: From Italian 'buona' via Polari secret language
From Polari, possibly French 'se camper' (to posture)
From 'ends' (plural of end) meaning local area

terms.definition: Uncool, tacky (possibly from Polari NAFF - Not Available For F***ing)
terms.etymology: Possibly Polari acronym or independent coinage

terms.context: Gaming, online chat - indicating temporary absence
terms.etymology: Internet acronym from early online gaming
From AstroTurf brand (fake grass) applied to fake movements
From legal 'bail' (release) extended to general leaving

terms.definition: Authentic, not caring about others' opinions
terms.etymology: Reclaimed by rapper Lil B from insult to compliment
Named after Twitch streamer Fanum's food-taking habit

terms.definition: Understand deeply, intuitively (from Heinlein's 'Stranger in Strange Land')
terms.etymology: Science fiction neologism from Robert Heinlein novel

terms.definition: Holding skateboard by trucks (marks as poser)
terms.etymology: Carrying method associated with mall shoppers, not real skaters
From Nerf toy brand (soft, harmless versions)

terms.definition: Non-Player Character; person without original thoughts

terms.etymology: From 'Play of the Game' acronym → Twitch emote
From 'pose' - pretending to be something you're not
Ballroom culture term for convincing presentation
Ballroom culture: 'throwing shade' like casting shadow

terms.definition: Nonsense word from viral video series
Experienced player using new account to play against beginners
terms.etymology: From original 'smurf' accounts created by pro players
Possibly from 'stoke' (fuel a fire) → fired up
